summary:
Midwives maintain equivalent competence whether practicing in urban or rural settings because their training includes comprehensive skills applicable universally. Historical and current certification standards support consistency in performance irrespective of geography.  

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{No}}$

Okay, I understand your request for only two concise sentences focusing solely on clinically significant points related to the query about midwifery competence in rural locations:

- The historical role of midwives as primary providers in rural communities demonstrates sustained effectiveness under resource-limited conditions without implying reduced technical ability. 
- Current national licensure requirements—such as those enabling certified nurse-midwives (CNMs) to independently prescribe medications nationwide—are designed to ensure standardized competencies across diverse environments including rural ones.
